description: The currently displayed image is an irrelevant software button marked with "Check for updates", consisting of a circular icon with an upper blue semi-ring, lower yellow semi-ring, and a red bookmark-like shape in the center, with the corresponding descriptive text below the icon. This image has no correlation with the provided research on Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steels, and no valid experimental data, microstructure characterization results, performance test parameters or other research-related information can be extracted from the current visible content.

This set of 4 optical micrographs shows the metallographic structures of Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels, with a uniform scale bar of 50 μm for all subgraphs. Subgraph (a) corresponds to M1-SS, the solution-treated Ti/V-free M1 steel, which consists of single-phase equiaxed austenite grains with an average size of 42.4 ± 7.8 μm and no precipitates. Subgraph (b) is M1-30, M1 steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h, with slightly grown equiaxed austenite grains (average size 45.3 ± 9.3 μm) and uniformly distributed nano-sized κ-carbides in the matrix. Subgraph (c) is M2-SS, the solution-treated Ti/V-modified M2 steel, showing significantly refined austenite grains with an average size of 32.3 ± 10.4 μm in bimodal distribution, plus uniformly dispersed (Ti,V)C nano-precipitates. Subgraph (d) is M2-30, M2 steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h, with austenite grains of 35.8 ± 12.7 μm, containing both (Ti,V)C precipitates and heterogeneously distributed κ-carbides nucleated around (Ti,V)C particles. The comparison demonstrates that Ti/V addition refines austenite grains, and aging treatment induces different κ-carbide precipitation behaviors in the two steels. This figure reveals the effects of alloy composition and heat treatment on metallographic microstructure, supporting the proposed microalloying strategy to regulate precipitates for optimized mechanical properties of low-density steels.

This figure presents two electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) inverse pole figure (IPF) maps for Ti/V-modified Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples under different heat treatment conditions. Subfigure (a) shows the IPF map of sample M2-SS, which underwent hot rolling followed by solution treatment at 1000 °C for 30 min and water quenching without aging treatment. Subfigure (b) shows the IPF map of sample M2-30, which received the same hot rolling and solution treatment as M2-SS, plus additional aging treatment at 550 °C for 30 h followed by water quenching. Both maps share a 50 μm scale bar, with grain orientations colored according to the standard IPF triangle corresponding to <001>, <101>, <111> crystallographic directions. M2-SS exhibits a bimodal austenite grain size distribution with an average grain size of 32.3 ± 10.4 μm, while M2-30 shows a unimodal grain size distribution with an average grain size of 35.8 ± 12.7 μm. No significant crystallographic texture is observed in either sample. These EBSD results characterize the grain structure features of the two samples, supporting subsequent analysis of the correlation between microstructure, precipitates and mechanical properties of the tested low-density steel.

This figure shows two bright-field (BF) transmission electron microscopy images of Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels after solution treatment, with a uniform scale bar of 1 μm. Subfigure (a) presents the microstructure of M1-SS, the Ti/V-free reference steel, which features clean, precipitate-free austenite grain boundaries and matrix, matching its single-phase austenite structure in the solution-treated state. Subfigure (b) displays the microstructure of M2-SS, the Ti/V-modified steel, where (Ti,V)C carbide precipitates are observed inside austenite grains and near grain boundaries as marked by blue arrows. The comparison confirms that Ti/V addition leads to the formation of (Ti,V)C precipitates during high-temperature processing even before aging, which supports the proposed microalloying strategy to regulate carbon distribution and subsequent κ-carbide precipitation for improved mechanical performance.

This figure displays bright-field (BF) transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dark-field (DF) TEM, and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of precipitates in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steels under different aging conditions. Subfigures (a-c) characterize M1-30 (Ti/V-free steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h), confirming uniformly distributed nanoscale κ-carbides in the austenite matrix. Subfigures (d-f) characterize M2-10 (Ti/V-modified steel aged at 550 °C for 10 h), identifying (Ti,V)C carbides and κ-carbides that preferentially nucleate at (Ti,V)C edges with nearly no matrix κ-carbides. Subfigures (g-j) characterize M2-30 (Ti/V-modified steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h), showing increased κ-carbide size and number density relative to M2-10, still predominantly located at (Ti,V)C boundaries. These results verify that pre-existing (Ti,V)C carbides act as preferred κ-carbide nucleation sites, suppressing homogeneous κ-carbide precipitation, supporting the microalloying strategy to tune precipitate distribution for optimized mechanical performance.

This figure presents the bright-field (BF) transmission electron microscopy image and corresponding energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping of sample M2-30, a Ti/V-modified Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h, with all subfigures using a 50 nm scale bar. The BF image marks the positions of κ-carbides (K) and (Ti,V)C precipitates. The remaining 8 subfigures show the spatial distribution of Fe, Mn, Al, Si, Cr, Mo, V, and Ti respectively. Obvious enrichment of V and Ti is observed at discrete nanoscale precipitates, corresponding to (Ti,V)C carbides, while matrix elements Fe, Mn, Al are uniformly distributed in the austenite matrix. These results verify the presence of (Ti,V)C precipitates, and support the conclusion that pre-existing (Ti,V)C acts as heterogeneous nucleation sites for κ-carbides to regulate precipitate distribution and optimize mechanical properties of the steel.

This set of figures presents TEM characterization results of the M2-30 sample, which is a Ti/V modified Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h. (a) is a bright-field (BF) TEM image with a 50 nm scale bar, showing (Ti,V)C precipitates in the austenite matrix and κ-carbides heterogeneously precipitated around the (Ti,V)C particles. (b) is a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image with a 10 nm scale bar, displaying the lattice structure of the matrix and precipitates. (c) is the fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of the selected region, acquired along the [110] zone axis, where diffraction spots of austenite γ (002), (Ti,V)C (002) and κ-carbide (001) are identified, confirming the orientation relationship (002)_(Ti,V)C || (001)_κ. These results verify that κ-carbides preferentially nucleate and grow at the edges of pre-existing (Ti,V)C carbides during aging, supporting the heterogeneous precipitation regulation mechanism for optimizing mechanical properties of the low-density steel.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels, including two subplots: (a) engineering stress-engineering strain curves, (b) true stress-true strain curves combined with strain hardening rate curves. Four tested samples are covered: M1-SS (Ti/V-free base steel after solution treatment), M1-30 (M1 steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h), M2-SS (Ti/V-modified steel after solution treatment), and M2-30 (M2 steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h). M2-30 exhibits the highest ultimate tensile strength (~1096 MPa), while M1-SS shows the highest total elongation (~80%). Aging treatment improves yield strength for both steel systems, and M2-30 retains a far higher strain hardening rate than M1-30 after aging. This figure verifies that Ti/V microalloying to regulate κ-carbide precipitation effectively balances strength and work hardening performance of the low-density steel.

This figure presents Electron Probe Microanalysis (EPMA) results for M2-SS, a solution-treated Ti/V-modified Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steel. Subfigure (a) shows the selected mapping domain, where an irregular (Ti, V)C carbide precipitate with size under 100 nm (marked by dashed line) is observed in the austenite matrix. Subfigure (b) exhibits the carbon element distribution around this (Ti, V)C carbide: carbon signal counts are highest at the core of (Ti, V)C, decrease gradually toward the carbide edge, and a surrounding carbon-rich region with higher carbon content than the matrix is confirmed. These results verify that pre-precipitated (Ti, V)C carbides act as carbon sinks to sequester carbon, modulating the residual carbon concentration in the matrix before aging treatment, thus regulating the heterogeneous precipitation of κ-carbides in subsequent aging processes.

This schematic diagram illustrates the precipitation behavior of M2 steel (Ti/V modified Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel) during aging at 550 °C. The left panel shows the austenite grain before aging, with pre-existing (Ti, V)C carbides inside: carbon content is highest at the core of (Ti, V)C carbides (red color) and decreases radially outwards (to yellow, green, blue). After aging at 550 °C, as shown in the right panel, κ-carbides preferentially nucleate and grow heterogeneously at the periphery of (Ti, V)C carbides, driven by the carbon distribution regulated by (Ti, V)C particles acting as carbon sinks. The legend identifies dark blue hexagons as austenite grains, colored concentric circles as (Ti, V)C carbides, and gray blocks as κ-carbides. This figure supports the core conclusion that Ti/V microalloying induces heterogeneous κ-carbide precipitation, avoiding the work hardening rate reduction caused by uniform κ-carbide precipitation in Ti/V-free low-density steels.

This stacked bar chart displays measured yield strength (red bars) and calculated contributions of different strengthening mechanisms to yield strength for four Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steel samples. The four samples are M1-SS (Ti/V-free M1 steel, solution treated without aging), M1-30 (M1 steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h), M2-SS (Ti/V-modified M2 steel, solution treated without aging), and M2-30 (M2 steel aged at 550 °C for 30 h). The y-axis represents strength in MPa. Strengthening contribution components include lattice friction strength σ₀ (blue), grain boundary strengthening σ_G (purple), Orowan strengthening σ_Or from (Ti,V)C precipitates (green), and shear strengthening σ_S from κ-carbides (yellow). Measured yield strength values are ~430 MPa for M1-SS, ~860 MPa for M1-30, ~650 MPa for M2-SS, and ~890 MPa for M2-30. Aging significantly improves yield strength for both steels; Ti/V addition introduces (Ti,V)C precipitates that provide Orowan strengthening, compensating for reduced κ-carbide shear strengthening to enhance overall yield strength, supporting the proposed Ti/V microalloying strategy for mechanical property optimization.

This figure contains two subplots (a and b) plotting work hardening index (ln(dσ/dε) as a function of lnε) for Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels. Subplot (a) presents data for Ti/V-free M1 steel, including solution-treated (M1-SS, grey square markers) and 550 °C aged for 30 h (M1-30, black downward triangle markers) samples: M1-SS shows three work hardening stages, while aged M1-30 only exhibits two stages, with Stage I as rapid ln(dσ/dε) decrease caused by fast dislocation slip, and Stage II as a stable plateau related to planar dislocation slip. Subplot (b) presents data for Ti/V-modified M2 steel, including solution-treated (M2-SS, pink square markers) and 550 °C aged for 30 h (M2-30, red downward triangle markers) samples, whose work hardening curves nearly overlap before and after aging. The results confirm that heterogeneous κ-carbide precipitation regulated by pre-existing (Ti,V)C carbides in M2 mitigates work hardening rate reduction after aging, supporting the Ti/V microalloying strategy to balance yield strength improvement and work hardening ability retention in low-density steels.

This figure contains three transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images capturing the microstructure of two Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples after tensile deformation: M1-30 (Ti/V-free, aged at 550 °C for 30 h, subplot a) and M2-30 (Ti/V-modified, aged at 550 °C for 30 h, subplots b and c). Subplot a (200 nm scale bar) shows slip bands and dislocation configurations in M1-30; subplot b (200 nm scale bar) exhibits significantly finer slip bands in M2-30; subplot c (30 nm scale bar, magnified region from b) reveals nanoscale non-shearable (Ti,V) carbides dispersed in M2-30 matrix. Both samples show planar dislocation slip as the primary deformation mode, consistent with calculated stacking fault energy values. Finer slip band spacing in M2-30 indicates more uniform severe deformation and higher work hardening index. The (Ti,V) carbides block dislocation motion to form high-dislocation-density regions that increase flow stress, while reduced homogeneous κ-carbide precipitation mitigates slip plane softening, supporting that Ti/V microalloying balances yield strength and work hardening performance of the steel.
